audit_items.proof_trace: In nfp_reprs_resync_phys_ports (line 535), invalid representors are cleaned via nfp_repr_clean(repr) which unregisters the netdev and frees dst/port, but never calls __nfp_repr_free — so repr->stats (percpu allocation from netdev_alloc_pcpu_stats) and the netdev memory (from alloc_etherdev_mqs) are not freed. No priv_destructor is registered anywhere in the NFP driver, so unregister_netdev alone does not reclaim these allocations; the correct call is nfp_repr_clean_and_free(repr), which all other teardown paths use.
audit_items.attack_scenario: An attacker with control over the NFP NIC firmware (or able to deliver traffic that causes the NSP to report physical ports as NFP_PORT_INVALID) can repeatedly trigger nfp_net_refresh_port_table_sync → nfp_reprs_resync_phys_ports, cycling port invalidation events to incrementally leak percpu stats and netdev memory and exhaust kernel memory.
